lessons_learned: A critical success factor for phasing-out ODS was the development of effective partnerships with the private sector and the customs service.

The process from design to promulgation of any new policy or regulation at the national level is a complicated process that takes at minimum 1-2 years. Interventions in this field must be prioritized in order to develop realistic implementation schedules. In this regard, awareness-raising of political decision makers is of great value for promotion of political relevance of the regulations under development that could accelerate the national consultation and enactment processes.

The main barriers to adoption of low-GWP alternative technologies to HCFCs include lack of knowledge and local expertise in the new technology, higher cost of the technology, and limited or no availability of equipment and servicing tools in the local market.

The remote conduct of this evaluation proved to be more demanding for timely organization of the planned virtual meetings as some stakeholders felt more freedom of choice that resulted in postponement of some interviews. Active involvement of UNDP MCO proved to be an important factor for organization of virtual meetings as the UN office can more easily convince national stakeholders and beneficiaries to adhere to the planned schedule of meetings with the evaluation team. Obviously, the assistance of the Implementing Agency should be restricted only to organization of meetings and not to data collection that would compromise independence of the evaluation.
recommendations: Recommendation 1: The Government should provide the necessary support for submission of a GEF project on assistance for ratification and implementation of the Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol in Azerbaijan.

Recommendation 2: The Government should consider adoption of necessary steps towards regulations supporting establishment of an official certification of Refrigeration and Air-Conditioning (RAC) service technicians.
Recommendation 3: The Government with cooperation of the main companies in the RAC sector should encourage establishment of a national RAC Association.

Recommendation 4: The Climate Change and Ozone Centre within the Ministry of Ecology and Natural Resources (MENR) should approach the UNEP ECA network for access to available standards and codes of good practice in the RAC sector.

Recommendation 5: The Climate Change and Ozone Centre within the MENR should consider development of outreach activities aiming at the end-users of RAC equipment to explain risks and disadvantages of engagements with the informal servicing sub-sector.

Recommendation 6: In the future projects for HFC phase-down, UNIDO should include sensitisation of the highest level of Government officials about the benefits of prioritization of legislative updates for HFC control.
Recommendation 7: In the future projects for HFC phase-down, UNIDO should include activities on demonstration of economic benefits of refrigerant recycling and reuse.
Recommendation 8: In the future project for HFC phase-down, UNIDO should include importers and distributors of HFC refrigerants and equipment in training and awareness raising activities.

Recommendation 9: In the future projects for HFC phase-down, UNIDO should ensure that indicators and targets are included in the project results framework for measurement and reporting on effectiveness of the national system for ODS control, e.g. indicators and targets for measurement of adherence to good practices in RAC servicing and for reporting of actual quantities of refrigerant recovery, re-use, and reclamation.

Recommendation 10: For future projects on HFC control, UNIDO should use the existing LTAs and eventually develop additional LTAs for procurement of equipment items of recurrent demand in order to reduce workload on administration of the procurement events and the time needed for acquisition of procured items.

Recommendation 11: For future GEF projects on ODS control, UNIDO should identify national partners in order to comply with the requirement for separation of the implementation and execution functions for the projects as stipulated in the GEF Updated Policy on Minimum Fiduciary Standards.

Recommendation 12: In preparation of the project on HFC control, UNIDO should pay due attention to proper formulation of the indicators and targets in the project results framework in order to facilitate M&E of the project.

Recommendation 13: UNIDO Management should ensure that for projects on ODS control a management response to the recommendations of Mid-term Reviews is prepared similar to the provisions of the UNIDO Evaluation Policy for independent evaluations.
executive_summary: This Independent Terminal Evaluation assessed a UNIDO project aimed at initiating the phase-out of hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) in the Republic of Azerbaijan in compliance with the Montreal Protocol. The project addressed two main components: strengthening institutional and regulatory frameworks for ODS control, and supporting technology conversion in the polyurethane foam and refrigeration sectors. Key achievements included the completion of HCFC-141b phase-out in the manufacturing sector, the establishment of a new methylformate-based polyol formulation facility, and the distribution of refrigerant recovery equipment to RAC service companies. The project also established a nationally recognized training and certification centre for RAC service technicians, developed an e-learning platform, and supported updates to national ODS legislation. On the institutional side, the project reinforced the capacity of customs officials and contributed to the development of a national licensing system. Despite these achievements, outcomes were constrained by delays in regulatory reform, absence of a formal RRR scheme, and limited data on environmental impact. The project's objective — achieving 99.5% HCFC phase-out by 2020 — was met according to mandatory Article 7 reports to the Ozone Secretariat. Overall ratings were Moderately Satisfactory for effectiveness and Moderately Likely for sustainability. The evaluation made 13 recommendations covering certification systems, RAC association establishment, legislative reform, refrigerant recycling, and improvements to future project design and monitoring.
thematic_justification: The project directly targeted the phase-out of ozone-depleting substances (HCFCs) through regulatory reform, technology conversion, and institutional capacity building, placing it squarely within the Chemicals & POPs thematic area.

sdg_mapping.13: The project contributed to SDG 13 by phasing out HCFCs — potent greenhouse gases — in the manufacturing and servicing sectors, directly reducing Azerbaijan's contribution to ozone depletion and climate change.
sdg_mapping.12: By promoting technology conversion to environmentally safer alternatives and establishing regulatory systems for ODS licensing and control, the project supports SDG 12 on responsible consumption and production.
sdg_mapping.17: The project was implemented through multilateral partnership between UNIDO, GEF, and national government agencies, reflecting the multi-stakeholder partnerships for sustainable development central to SDG 17.

context.title: Independent Terminal Evaluation: Initiation of the HCFC Phase Out in the Republic of Azerbaijan
context.donor: Multilateral Fund for the Implementation of the Montreal Protocol
